case_name: Natural organic matter reduction from colored surface water for drinking water production
task_description: Recommend a ranked list of feasible treatment process chains for natural-organic-matter-rich surface water intended for drinking water supply.

input_data.scenario_background: A small town draws drinking water from a reservoir with recurring color, elevated dissolved organic carbon, and seasonal algae influence. The utility wants a proven treatment train that reliably reduces natural organic matter and supports downstream disinfection without excessive disinfection by-product formation. The plant can manage standard chemical dosing, solids separation, and filter operation, but it prefers stable and conventional processes over highly specialized systems.

treatment_goal.target_requirement: Finished water should achieve reliable natural organic matter reduction, lower color and DOC/TOC to support compliance with drinking water quality and disinfection by-product control objectives, and provide microbiologically safe potable water.
secondary_goals: use well-established drinking water treatment technology
maintain stable operation across seasonal raw water variation
limit energy-intensive advanced treatment where not necessary
keep residuals handling manageable for a small municipal utility

engineering_constraints.residuals_management: conventional sludge handling and periodic backwash waste management are available

core_unit_functions.enhanced_coagulation: destabilize particles and maximize removal of humic natural organic matter and color through optimized coagulant and pH control
core_unit_functions.flocculation: grow settleable or floatable flocs containing organic matter and suspended solids
core_unit_functions.sedimentation_or_dissolved_air_flotation: separate formed flocs and reduce particulate and algae-associated load
core_unit_functions.rapid_sand_or_dual_media_filtration: polish remaining particles and protect final disinfection performance
core_unit_functions.disinfection: provide microbiological safety after organic matter reduction

applicability_rationale: enhanced coagulation is a standard and widely supported approach for NOM-rich colored surface waters used for drinking water
the raw water shows elevated DOC, UV254, color, and SUVA, indicating a substantial fraction of coagulation-removable humic material
surface water with moderate turbidity and seasonal algae fits conventional clarification plus filtration treatment
this train directly supports DBP precursor reduction while remaining proven and robust for municipal operation
potential_risks: suboptimal coagulant dose or pH control can significantly reduce NOM removal
seasonal algae peaks may favor dissolved air flotation over plain settling in some periods
sludge production increases with enhanced coagulation and requires routine management

core_unit_functions.coagulation: remove particulate and coagulation-responsive organic matter
core_unit_functions.flocculation: form buoyant flocs suitable for flotation separation
core_unit_functions.dissolved_air_flotation: improve removal of algae, low-density flocs, and associated organic matter
core_unit_functions.granular_media_filtration: polish solids carryover and stabilize downstream adsorber performance
core_unit_functions.granular_activated_carbon: adsorb residual dissolved organic compounds and improve taste, odor, and precursor reduction

applicability_rationale: useful where seasonal algae and residual dissolved organics justify additional polishing beyond conventional clarification
GAC can complement coagulation by removing portions of dissolved organics that remain after clarification
DAF is a strong clarification option for reservoir waters influenced by algae and lighter floc
potential_risks: higher media replacement or reactivation cost than conventional filtration alone
GAC performance declines as adsorption sites are exhausted
more process steps increase operator attention and monitoring needs

core_unit_functions.coagulation: aggregate particulate matter and remove a portion of NOM
core_unit_functions.flocculation: increase floc size for solid-liquid separation
core_unit_functions.sedimentation: remove bulk floc solids prior to filtration
core_unit_functions.rapid_sand_filtration: remove remaining suspended matter and improve finished water clarity
core_unit_functions.powdered_activated_carbon_as_needed: provide flexible supplemental adsorption during episodic high color, taste, odor, or elevated organics events

applicability_rationale: a standard conventional treatment train can be effective when supported by optimized coagulant control
PAC offers event-based flexibility without a full fixed-bed adsorber system
this is practical for utilities seeking moderate cost and familiar operation
potential_risks: conventional coagulation without enhanced optimization may not achieve as much DOC reduction as the top-ranked option
PAC is less predictable for sustained bulk NOM control than enhanced coagulation or GAC
additional solids loading from PAC can affect sludge production and filter performance

core_unit_functions.ozonation_or_biofiltration_pretreatment: transform some organic fractions and improve biodegradability where ozone is used, or begin biological degradation where biofiltration is established
core_unit_functions.biological_activated_carbon_or_biologica: biodegrade and adsorb biodegradable dissolved organic matter and provide polishing

applicability_rationale: can reduce biodegradable organics and provide good finished water quality when biological operation is well managed
relevant where the utility also values taste and odor control and longer-term organic polishing
biofiltration aligns with known natural and biological transformation mechanisms for organic matter
potential_risks: ozonation can increase biodegradable byproducts and requires careful downstream biological control
biological systems need more process monitoring and stable nutrient and hydraulic conditions
not the simplest first-choice option for an easy case centered on proven conventional operation

core_unit_functions.ultrafiltration_or_nanofiltration: provide physical barrier removal of particles and, for tighter membranes, partial to strong rejection of dissolved organic matter
core_unit_functions.activated_carbon_or_post_stabilization: polish residual dissolved organics or condition treated water quality

applicability_rationale: membranes can technically treat surface waters and improve finished water consistency
nanofiltration can reduce dissolved organic matter more strongly than conventional particle barriers
potential_risks: higher capital and operating cost than needed for a straightforward NOM-focused surface water case
membrane fouling risk is significant with organic-rich and algae-influenced raw water
concentrate or residual stream management can be burdensome, especially for tighter membranes

preferred_rank_pattern: enhanced_coagulation_plus_clarification_plus_filtration should rank first for NOM-rich surface water under moderate constraints
conventional clarification and filtration with optional carbon polishing should rank above high-energy membrane options
membrane-heavy solutions should not be ranked first when the main problem is bulk natural organic matter and color

must_reflect: preference for stable and proven municipal drinking water processes
operator capability limited to conventional low-to-medium complexity systems
residuals handling should remain within normal sludge and backwash management practice

must_contain: why enhanced coagulation is appropriate for DOC, UV254, color, and DBP precursor reduction
how surface water matrix effects such as algae and turbidity influence clarification selection
why conventional treatment ranks above advanced membrane treatment in this scenario

should_mention: coagulant dose and pH sensitivity for NOM removal
seasonal algae impacts on clarification and filtration
sludge production and residuals from coagulation
membrane fouling or unnecessary complexity for lower-ranked options
